coredata.dc:title: Influence of Nanobubble Concentration on Blood–Brain Barrier Opening Using Focused Ultrasound Under Real-Time Acoustic Feedback Control
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2019 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Inc. on behalf of World Federation for Ultrasound in Medicine & Biology.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Inc. on behalf of World Federation for Ultrasound in Medicine & Biology.
coredata.dc:creator: Cheng, Bingbing
Bing, Chenchen
Xi, Yin
Shah, Bhavya
Exner, Agata A.
Chopra, Rajiv
coredata.dc:description: Real-time acoustic feedback control based on harmonic emissions of stimulated microbubbles may serve as a way to achieve reliable blood–brain barrier (BBB) opening with focused ultrasound in the brain. Previously, we demonstrated BBB opening was possible using sub-micron bubbles (aka nanobubbles) and produced comparable results to commercially available microbubbles (Optison, Definity, etc.). The harmonic emissions and acoustic control were observed to be more consistent using nanobubbles, which warrants further study of BBB opening using these agents. This study examined the stimulated acoustic emissions of nanobubbles at different concentrations both in vitro and in vivo and evaluated BBB opening under real-time acoustic feedback control across concentrations. Original nanobubbles (1011 bubbles/mL) have long in vitro persistence (7.3 ± 3.3 min) and circulation time in rats (approximately 10 min) under exposures in this study, and both degraded with dilutions. With all three tested dilutions (1:1, 1:10 and 1:100), successful BBB opening was reliably achieved under real-time feedback control.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Blood–brain barrier
Focused ultrasound
Nanobubble
Concentration
Acoustic
Harmonic emission
Feedback control

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Ultrasound system and acoustic detection algorithm
In vitro acoustic characterization of nanobubbles
In vivo acoustic characterization of nanobubbles
In vitro characterization of nanobubble acoustic emissions
In vivo characterization of nanobubble acoustic emissions
BBB opening using FUS and nanobubbles under real-time feedback control

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Experimental setup. (a) Components of the focused ultrasound system used in this study, including a focused ultrasound transducer driving subsystem and a hydrophone-based acoustic feedback subsystem. (b) In vitro tube phantom setup. (c) In vivo FUS setup for transcranial exposures. The acoustic emissions from the stimulated nanobubbles are measured with a confocal hydrophone and digitized by the computer. The frequency spectrum of the acoustic emissions is analyzed, and the output signal to the transducer is adjusted based on the integrated power across a harmonic band. For the in vitro study, a single tube phantom was used to evaluate the bubble response. A rat model was used for the in vivo study. BBB = blood–brain barrier; FUS = focused ultrasound; RF = radio frequency.

Nanobubble structure and characterization. (a) A schematic diagram of the synthesized nanobubbles, which were composed of lipids DBPC, DPPA, DPPE and mPEG-DSPE and surfactant molecules Pluronic with a hydrophobic gas core of C3F8. (b, c) Representative size distribution and concentration measured by DLS and the qNano platform. C3F8 = octafluoropropane; DBPC = 1,2-dibehenoyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphocholine; DLS = dynamic light scattering; DPPA = 1,2-dipalmitoyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphate; DPPE = 1,2-dipalmitoyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphoethanolamine; mPEG-DSPE = 1,2-distearoyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphoethanolamine-N-[methoxy(polyethyleneglycol)-2000] (ammonium salt); PEG = polyethylene glycol.

In vitro characterization of acoustic emissions from stimulated nanobubbles. (a) Acoustic response (AUC) as a function of US focal pressure with a nanobubble concentration at 1:1. (b) Relationship between the AUC and the different bubble concentrations under a fixed ultrasound pressure of 1.13 MPa. Unlike microbubbles, the signal only dropped for dilutions greater than 1:2000. (c) Persistence time as a function of bubble concentration under a fixed ultrasound pressure of 1.13 MPa. (d) In vitro bubble stability at different concentrations. *p < 0.0001 (compared with T0). AUC = area under the curve; US = ultrasonography.

In vivo characterization of acoustic emissions from stimulated nanobubbles. (a) AUC of stimulated nanobubbles as a function of US focal pressure at concentrations 1:1 (n = 4 in two animals), 1:10 (n = 8 in two animals) and 1:100 (n = 6 in two animals), respectively. (b) Broadband emission of 1:10 NB as a function of ultrasound focal pressure (n = 8 in two animals). (c) Representative acoustic frequency spectrum as a function of ultrasound pressure for 1:10 NB. The left is the full spectra, and the right show three detailed frequency spectra at 0.22, 0.31 and 0.70 MPa, respectively. (d) In vivo circulation time of nanobubbles at three dilutions 1:1 (n = 3 in three animals), 1:10 (n = 3 in three animals) and 1:100 (n = 3 in three animals) after a bolus injection of 0.15–0.23 mL (pressure: 0.54 MPa). *p < 0.05. Error bar: Standard deviation. AUC = area under the curve; NB = nanobubble; US = ultrasound.

BBB opening under feedback control with nanobubbles in a rat model with three different dilutions: 1:1 (a, d), 1:10 (b, e) and 1:100 (c, f). Top panel: Representative feedback control signal (green solid, left y-axis) and ultrasound focal pressure (orange circle, right y-axis) as a function of time from one target; bottom panel: Gross-tissue photo of the brain slice with Evans blue leakage indicating the opening. Scale bar: 5 mm. The feedback control threshold was set to be 5000. All three dilutions showed a good feedback control performance and a successful BBB opening effect. However, compared with 1:1 and 1:10 NBs, the control viability was a little larger for 1:100 nanobubbles. BBB = blood–brain barrier; NB = nanobubble.
A summary table of the AUC values and focal pressures during the BBB opening experiment for the three bubble concentrations

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: AUC and pressure values are presented as means and standard deviations.
AUC = area under the curve; BBB = blood–brain barrier.

head.ce:title: Influence of Nanobubble Concentration on Blood–Brain Barrier Opening Using Focused Ultrasound Under Real-Time Acoustic Feedback Control

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Radiology, UT Southwestern Medical Center, Dallas, TX, USA
Department of Clinical Science, UT Southwestern Medical Center, Dallas, TX, USA
Advanced Imaging Research Center, UT Southwestern Medical Center, Dallas, TX, USA
Department of Radiology, Case Western Reserve University, Cleveland, OH, USA

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Address correspondence to: Bingbing Cheng, Department of Radiology, University of Texas Southwestern Medical Center, 5323 Harry Hines Blvd, Dallas, TX 75390, USA.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Texas Southwestern Medical Center

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Real-time acoustic feedback control based on harmonic emissions of stimulated microbubbles may serve as a way to achieve reliable blood–brain barrier (BBB) opening with focused ultrasound in the brain. Previously, we demonstrated BBB opening was possible using sub-micron bubbles (aka nanobubbles) and produced comparable results to commercially available microbubbles (Optison, Definity, etc.). The harmonic emissions and acoustic control were observed to be more consistent using nanobubbles, which warrants further study of BBB opening using these agents. This study examined the stimulated acoustic emissions of nanobubbles at different concentrations both in vitro and in vivo and evaluated BBB opening under real-time acoustic feedback control across concentrations. Original nanobubbles (1011 bubbles/mL) have long in vitro persistence (7.3 ± 3.3 min) and circulation time in rats (approximately 10 min) under exposures in this study, and both degraded with dilutions. With all three tested dilutions (1:1, 1:10 and 1:100), successful BBB opening was reliably achieved under real-time feedback control.

ce:section.ce:para: The blood–brain barrier (BBB) is a physiologic, semipermeable and selective barrier that separates circulating blood from the extracellular fluid in the central nervous system (CNS). Through tight junctions between endothelial cells and a number of transporters (e.g., efflux transporters and nutrient transporters), the BBB controls the movement of ions and molecules between the blood and the brain, maintains brain homeostasis and protects the brain from toxins, pathogens, inflammation, injury and disease (Daneman and Prat 2015). However, the BBB also presents a barrier for the efficient delivery of therapeutic agents into the brain to treat brain tumors or other neurodegenerative disorders (Abbott and Romero 1996; Daneman and Prat 2015; Dasgupta et al. 2016; Pardridge 2005; Qureshi and Chiocca 1999). Because of the presence of the BBB, nearly 100% of systemically administered drugs larger than 400 Da are excluded from the CNS, thus significantly limiting drug delivery to the brain (Pardridge 2007).

Efforts have been made to circumvent the BBB by either modification of drugs or drug carriers (Juillerat-Jeanneret 2008; Larsen et al. 2014) or disruption of the BBB (Doolittle et al. 2000; Hynynen et al. 2001; Kung et al. 2018; Neuhaus et al. 2018; Vykhodtseva et al. 1995). Currently, focused ultrasound (FUS) combined with microbubbles is the only method capable of local, non-invasive and transient opening of the BBB (Bing et al. 2014; Choi et al. 2007; Chopra et al. 2010; Hynynen et al. 2001; Liu et al. 2008; McDannold et al. 2005; O'Reilly et al. 2011; Samiotaki et al. 2012; Wu et al. 2017). A broad spectrum of therapeutic molecules have been successfully delivered to the brain parenchyma after ultrasound exposure, including chemotherapeutics (Fan et al. 2013, 2014; Treat et al. 2012), therapeutic agents for immunotherapy (Kinoshita et al. 2006; Wilcock et al. 2004), viral vectors for gene therapy (Burgess et al. 2012; Wang et al. 2015) and stem cells (Burgess et al. 2011). A good safety profile has been demonstrated not only in small animals but also in non-human primates (Downs et al. 2015; McDannold et al. 2012). To date, several initial clinical investigations in patients with brain tumors are either underway or completed to determine the feasibility and safety of BBB opening using FUS in humans, further advancing this technique to the clinic (Carpentier et al. 2016; Lipsman et al. 2017, 2018).

The influence of multiple physical parameters on the magnitude and duration of BBB opening using FUS combined with microbubbles has been comprehensively studied in past years, including (i) ultrasound exposure related parameters (e.g., pressure, frequency, pulse length, pulse repetition frequency and duration) (Chopra et al. 2010; McDannold et al. 2008a, 2008b), and (ii) contrast agent related parameters (e.g., microbubble injection rate [O'Reilly et al. 2011], dosage of microbubbles [McDannold et al. 2008b; Treat et al. 2007; Yang et al. 2008], microbubble size [Choi et al. 2010; Samiotaki et al. 2012; Tung et al. 2011] and microbubble formulation [Choi et al. 2010; Samiotaki et al. 2012; Sirsi and Borden 2009]). Among those studies, it has been demonstrated that ultrasound contrast agents (microbubbles) play a significant role in BBB opening. McMahon et al. recently reported that the injected concentration of microbubbles is important, and high microbubble dose can be associated with neuroinflammation (McMahon and Hynynen 2017). Prior studies have investigated the influence of microbubble size using size-isolation techniques, but this has been over a limited range of sizes in the micron range (Choi et al. 2010; Samiotaki et al. 2012; Tung et al. 2011). To date, only a few studies have investigated the utility or impact of sub-micron bubble agents on BBB opening (Bing et al. 2018; Fan et al. 2014; Huang et al. 2015). Microbubbles are not necessarily optimized for BBB opening, and there are persistent challenges with short lifetime, heterogeneous perfusion and sensitivity to handling. Recently, nanometer-sized bubbles (referred to as nanobubbles) have been developed as a new type of ultrasound contrast agent to enhance the multifunctional contrast and delivery capacity. They demonstrate unique properties for ultrasound imaging and are comparable or superior to commercially available microbubbles in many aspects (Gao et al. 2017; Krupka et al. 2010; Perera et al. 2015; Wu et al. 2013).

Previously, our group developed a portable ultrasound system integrated into a standard stereotaxic platform and demonstrated its ability to open the BBB in rodents (Bing et al. 2014; Mulik et al. 2016). The ultrasound system was integrated into a standard stereotaxic neuroscience platform to facilitate the study of BBB opening in neuroscience research groups. Recently, the system was extended to include a hydrophone to monitor acoustic emissions from stimulated bubble agents for real-time acoustic feedback control (Bing et al. 2018). Using this system, it was observed that BBB opening could be achieved with commercially available microbubble agents (Optison [GE Healthcare, Milwaukee, WI, USA] and Definity [Lantheus Medical Imaging, North Billerica, MA, USA]) and with custom-made nanobubbles. This initial investigation also revealed that many acoustic properties of the stimulated nanobubbles were distinct from microbubbles and deserve further study.

This study aimed to characterize the stimulated acoustic emissions of nanobubbles at different concentrations both in vitro and in vivo and evaluate their BBB opening performance under real-time acoustic feedback control. The in vitro characterization included the acoustic signal strength as a function of ultrasound pressure, the persistence and the time-dependent stability. The in vivo acoustic response of stimulated nanobubbles as well as their circulation time at three bubble concentrations (1:1, 1:10 and 1:100) were characterized in rats. Finally, the feasibility of BBB opening at the selected concentrations was evaluated under real-time feedback control. The information gleaned from these studies will help select optimal configurations for using nanobubbles with FUS to open the BBB.

Lipids 1,2-dibehenoyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphocholine (DBPC), 1,2-dipalmitoyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphate (DPPA) and 1,2-dipalmitoyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphoethanolamine (DPPE) were purchased from Avanti Polar Lipids (Alabaster, AL, USA). 1,2-Distearoyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphoethanolamine-N-[methoxy(polyethyleneglycol)-2000] (ammonium salt) (mPEG-DSPE) was obtained from Laysan Lipids (Arab, AL, USA). Pluronic L10 was obtained from BASF Corporation (Florham Park, NJ, USA), and glycerol was purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific (Waltham, MA, USA). All chemicals were used directly without further purification. Octafluoropropane (C3F8) gas was obtained from FluoroMed, L.P. (Round Rock, TX, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ultrasound system and acoustic detection algorithm
ce:section.ce:para: The ultrasound system and acoustic feedback control algorithm were similar to our previous report (Bing et al. 2018) as shown in Figure 1 a. Briefly, a single-element focused lead zirconate titanate (PZT) transducer, which was custom-made (DL-54, DeL Piezo Specialties, LLC, West Palm Beach, FL, USA) with a 75-mm diameter and a 60-mm radius of curvature, was used to transmit ultrasound energy to the target. The fundamental frequency of the transducer was 0.5 MHz as measured with an impedance analyzer (C-60, Cypher Instruments, London, UK). The spatial pressure distribution at the transducer focus and the pressure-voltage relationship were characterized in a hydrophone tank with a needle hydrophone (SN2344, Precision Acoustic, Dorchester, Dorset, UK). A 3-D-printed cone filled with degassed water was attached to the transducer and enabled coupling of the ultrasound beam to the target phantom or rat brain. The transducer was operated at the resonant frequency of 0.5 MHz and was connected to a custom-built driving system and stereotaxic apparatus (51730 M, Stoelting Co., Wood Dale, IL, USA). A hydrophone, which was constructed from a flat 20-mm diameter PZT composite material (DL-53 1-3 composite, center frequency 0.75 MHz, DeL Piezo Specialties, LLC), was inserted into a 26-mm diameter aperture in the center of the transducer to detect the stimulated acoustic emissions of nanobubbles. During each ultrasound exposure (10 ms burst, 1 Hz repetition frequency), acoustic emissions detected with the hydrophone underwent a fast Fourier transform to reveal the frequency domain information. The area under the curve (AUC) was calculated by summing the amplitude at the ultra-harmonic frequency (0.75 MHz) within a 100 kHz bandwidth. During feedback control, the average AUC value from the last three acquisitions was compared with a desired AUC threshold and the pressure for the next ultrasound exposure was adjusted accordingly using a step size of 0.01–0.03 MPa.

The nanobubble synthesis protocol was modified from a previously published protocol (Hernandez et al. 2018). The modified procedure is described here. DBPC, DPPA, DPPE and mPEG-DSPE were dissolved in 8 mL chloroform with a mass ratio of 6:1:2:1 in a glass vial. To facilitate the dissolution, the lipid solution was placed within a round-bottom flask in a 37˚C water bath while rotating at a speed of approximately 112 revolutions per minute (RPM) for 1 h (Rotavapor R-210, BUCHI, New Castle, DE, USA). Once dissolved, the solution was split into 3-mL glass vials and was stored in a vacuum chamber for 24 h to ensure complete solvent evaporation. Then 50 µL of glycerol and 1 mL of phosphate buffered saline containing 0.6 mg/mL Pluronic L10 were added into each vial, and the vials were immediately sealed. Hydration of the lipid films took place at 80˚C for 1 h in a digital water bath (PB-600, Boekel, Feasterville, PA, USA). Once hydrated, the air inside the vial was replaced with C3F8 gas. Finally, the vial was placed on a Vial-Mix shaker (Bristol-Myers Squibb Medical Imaging, Inc., N. Billerica, MA, USA) to form the nanobubbles. The activated vials were centrifuged upside-down at 50 g relative centrifugal force for 5 min, and the nanobubble solution was carefully drawn out from the bottom of the vial with an 18-gauge syringe.

The size and the concentration of the nanobubbles were characterized using a dynamic light scattering (DLS) instrument (Zetasizer Nano ZS, Malvern Panalytical, Worcestershire, UK) and a qNano platform from Izon Science (Christchurch, New Zealand). The qNano was equipped with the NP300 nanopore for detection of particles in the range of 150–900 nm. The optimized parameters of the qNano were adjusted (45 mm stretch, 0.7 mV current) to keep the baseline current close to 130 nA. Then, 40 μL of diluted nanobubble solution (dilution factor: 5000) was loaded to the upper fluid cell, and pressure (16 mbar) was applied to obtain the desired 500 particle count. The original bubble concentration was calculated by multiplying the measured concentration with the dilution factor (5000). The size and concentration distribution plots were created by Izon Control Suite software, version 2.1 (Izon Science).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro acoustic characterization of nanobubbles
ce:section.ce:para: Non-diethylhexyl phthalate polyvinyl chloride tubing with an inner diameter of 0.65 mm and an outer diameter of 1 mm (EXT-12 HF, SAI Infusion Technologies, Lake Villa, IL, USA) was placed in a water tank across the ultrasound focus (Fig. 1b). The nanobubble solution was infused continuously using an magnetic resonance imaging (MRI)-compatible syringe pump (Chemyx NanoJet Stereotaxic Syringe Pump, Chemyx, Stafford, TX, USA) at a rate of 0.15 mL/min to refresh the bubble population within the focal volume of the ultrasound after each pulse. The initial nanobubble concentration was 1011 bubbles/mL. To investigate the relationship between the nanobubble acoustic response and the ultrasound pressure at different dilutions (1:1, 1:10, 1:100, 1:500, 1:1000, 1:2000, 1:8000, 1:16000 and 1:100000), a pressure sweep was performed. In the pressure sweep, the focal pressure was increased from 0.21–1.13 MPa with a step size of 0.01–0.03 MPa. At each pressure level, at least three waveforms were acquired, and the AUC values were captured and averaged to represent the acoustic emission at this specific pressure level. When comparing the acoustic emissions across different dilutions, the AUC values (the strength of acoustic emissions) were recorded at a fixed ultrasound pressure of 1.13 MPa. Three independent tests were performed, and all nine dilutions were evaluated accordingly.

The persistence of stimulated acoustic emissions was another property of nanobubbles that was evaluated. It was defined as the time to reduce the harmonic response from the stimulated bubbles to baseline levels with stationary flow. At a fixed focal pressure, the acoustic emissions generated from the stationary nanobubbles will decrease over time as each successive transmitted ultrasound pulse destroys a fraction of the bubble population. This property should depend on the starting bubble concentration. In this study, the focal pressure was fixed at 1.13 MPa, and the ultrasound pulses were continuously delivered into the tube without flow. Nanobubble samples with different dilutions were evaluated in three independent tests.

Time dependent stability, indicated by the signal strength of the acoustic emissions as a function of time after activation, was evaluated by repeating the pressure sweep measurements at different time points and recording the maximum AUC value. The first measurement was performed immediately after the sample was made. Two more measurements were performed at 1 and 2 h afterward to monitor the signal decrease. Five different dilutions were tested (1:1, 1:2, 1:10, 1:100 and 1:1000) to cover a reasonable concentration range.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo acoustic characterization of nanobubbles

ce:section.ce:para: Female rats (Sprague Dawley, 230–300 g, n = 40) were used in this study. All procedures were approved by the UT Southwestern Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee and followed guidelines set forth by the Guide for the Care and Use of Laboratory Animals. Animals were anesthetized through inhalation using a mixture of 2%–3% isoflurane and 1–2 L/min of 100% oxygen. A 24-gauge intravenous catheter was placed in the lateral tail vein for drug administration. A physiologic monitoring system (PhysioSuite, Kent Scientific Corp., Torrington, CT, USA) was used to monitor vital signs and maintain core body temperature throughout the experiment. Hair over the cranial surface of the skull was removed using an animal clipper and depilatory cream (Veet sensitive formula, Reckitt Benckiser, Parsippany, NJ, USA) to enable ultrasound propagation into the brain. After preparation, the animal was transferred to the stereotaxic apparatus and stabilized using ear bars and a bite bar. A custom-built nose cone was placed over the animal's nose to deliver inhalant anesthetic during sonications.

A small skin incision was made over the skull to identify cranial landmarks for atlas registration. The registration procedure was performed using the method described previously (Bing et al. 2014). The ultrasound setup for the animal study is shown in Figure 1c. Ultrasound gel was applied on top of the skin incision to allow the penetration of the ultrasound beam. A water tank filled with degassed water was adapted to the stereotaxic apparatus for coupling. For indication of the BBB opening, Evans blue (EB, 3.33 mL/kg dosage) was injected via the tail vein catheter and allowed to circulate for a minimum of 3 min. Nanobubbles were administered through the tail vein catheter either via bolus or infusion injection, according to different sonication protocols. Animals were euthanized by exsanguination using transcardial perfusion with saline and 10% buffered formalin approximately 10 min after sonication. The brain was harvested immediately and placed in formalin for final fixation.

Three types of acoustic characterization were performed in vivo: acoustic emissions of nanobubble samples at different dilutions as a function of focal pressure (n = 6, 18 measurements); in vivo circulation time of nanobubble samples at different dilutions (n = 9, nine measurements); and BBB opening effect using feedback control with nanobubble samples at different dilutions (n = 25, 64 measurements).

The in vivo acoustic emissions as a function of focal pressure were evaluated in a similar manner to the in vitro experiments. A pressure sweep was performed in vivo with nanobubble samples at three different dilutions: 1:1, 1:10 and 1:100. In each experiment, the nanobubble samples were continuously infused through the tail vein with an MRI-compatible syringe pump (Chemyx NanoJet Stereotaxic Syringe Pump, Chemyx). The infusion rate was set to be 0.3 mL/min for adequate signal to noise ratio and to limit the total volume of infusion into the rat. Each pressure sweep experiment was done in a new brain location or a new rat. The focal pressure was increased from 0.14 to 0.76 MPa, with an approximate step size of 0.01–0.03 MPa. The duration of each pressure sweep was approximately 120 s. Broadband emission was evaluated based on the frequency spectrum of each acquisition. The total power of the emission signal centered at 1.15 MHz with a bandwidth of 25 kHz was calculated as an indicator of broadband emission.

In vivo circulation time, defined as the time from max ultra-harmonic emission to baseline level after a bolus injection was evaluated for three dilutions as well (1:1, 1:10 and 1:100). The nanobubble was injected via a bolus injection at doses of 737, 73.7 and 7.37 µL/kg, respectively. The dosage of 737 µL/kg for the undiluted solution (1:1) was chosen according to our previous study to obtain similar gas volume with commercialized microbubbles (Optison and Definity). The half-life of the nanobubble (1:10) was calculated based on the circulation time curve (ultra-harmonic emissions). Ultrasound exposures were initiated as soon as the nanobubble bolus injection started. The focal pressure was selected to be 0.54 MPa as a conventional pressure for BBB opening.

The feedback-controlled sonication was applied in vivo to evaluate the BBB opening performance of nanobubble samples with different dilutions (1:1, 1:10 and 1:100). The treatment duration was fixed at 100 s according to a previous report (Bing et al. 2018). To evaluate the controller performance, the mean AUC and pressure were calculated both over time (100 s in each treatment) and sonications across multiple targets and summarized in Table 1 . The acoustic performance data in this table was obtained from ongoing projects with different targets and animals in three concentration groups.

Results are presented as means and standard deviations. All the tests in this study were repeated at least three times independently (n ≥ 3). Analysis of variance was performed in the nanobubble in vitro stability test. Linear mixed model was used in the nanobubble in vivo pressure sweep experiment. Repeated measurements were accounted for using a random effect. Tukey adjustment was used to adjust for multiple comparison. A p value of less than 0.05 (p < 0.05) was considered significant. All analyses were performed in SAS 9.4 (SAS Institute Inc., Cary, NC, USA).

Lipid-shelled nanobubbles were successfully synthesized by incorporating a surfactant molecule Pluronic. As shown in Figure 2 a, the shell was composed of lipids DBPC, DPPA, DPPE, mPEG-DSPE and Pluronic L10. The core was filled with C3F8 gas. The bubbles had an average diameter of 288.0 ± 17.6 nm (n = 6) with a polydispersity index of 0.32 ± 0.05 measured by the DLS (Fig. 2b). The histogram of nanobubble size distribution acquired using the qNano platform is shown in Figure 2c. The results indicated a similar mean bubble diameter of 279.2 ± 5.8 nm compared with DLS results and a concentration of 2.9 ± 0.3 × 1011 bubbles/mL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro characterization of nanobubble acoustic emissions
ce:section.ce:para: Figure 3 a shows the relationship between the acoustic response (AUC) of stimulated nanobubbles (original concentration) and the ultrasound focal pressure. During the ultrasound pressure sweep (0.21–1.13 MPa), the AUC of the stimulated nanobubbles increased with increasing ultrasound pressure. The nanobubbles’ acoustic response as a function of dilution was plotted and compared, as shown in Figure 3b. Interestingly, the AUC didn't decrease immediately with the dilution when the nanobubbles were diluted from 1:1 (1011 bubbles/mL) to 1:2000 (107–108 bubbles/mL). After being diluted to greater than 2000 times, the AUC of the nanobubbles started decreasing along with the concentration decrease. For bubbles at raw concentration, the persistence time was 439.7 ± 198.8 s, as shown in Figure 3c, but dropped quickly with increasing dilutions. For example, the persistence reduced to 49.6% (218.3 ± 121.9 s) and 13.6% (59.7 ± 16.7 s) at 1:10 and 1:100 dilutions, respectively. The stability measurements indicated that nanobubbles were stable for at least 2 h up to dilutions of 1:10, as shown in Figure 3d. When the dilution dropped to 1:100 or below, the stability started to degrade quickly. For example, the acoustic response of nanobubbles with 1:100 dilution at 1 h after the sample was prepared was significantly lower than that of fresh sample (73%, p < 0.0001).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo characterization of nanobubble acoustic emissions
ce:section.ce:para: The relationship between the acoustic response and ultrasound focal pressure at bubble dilutions 1:1, 1:10 and 1:100 in rats is shown in Figure 4 a. The results showed a pressure-dependent AUC increase as expected, however, no significant difference in the signal strength was observed among those three groups. The mean AUC values under each ultrasound pressure of 1:1 and 1:100 samples were very similar and slightly lower than those of 1:10 samples. There were no measurable differences among the three groups except that the 1:10 samples had significantly higher AUC than the 1:100 samples at 0.74 (p = 0.04) and 0.76 MPa (p = 0.03) on average. The focal pressure was varied from 0.14–0.76 MPa, factoring in skull attenuation. As 1:10 nanobubbles have a slightly relatively higher acoustic response, the broadband emission during the pressure sweep was evaluated using the 1:10 samples as an example and is shown in Figure 4b. There was no significant broadband emission below 0.5 MPa. Acoustic frequency spectrum for the 1:10 nanobubbles at different pressure exposures is shown in Figure 4c, which shows the acoustic response of the stimulated nanobubbles at different frequencies with respect to ultrasound pressure. Figure 4c also shows three specific frequency spectra at 0.22, 0.31 and 0.70 MPa, indicating the background (0.22 MPa), presence of ultra-harmonic (0.31 MPa) and broadband emissions (0.70 MPa) as an example. Figure 4d shows the in vivo circulation time of the nanobubbles at dilutions 1:1, 1:10 and 1:100. For the undiluted nanobubbles, the in vivo circulation time from max ultra-harmonic emission to baseline level was around 10 min. It reduced to about 5–6 min when the concentration was diluted to 1:10 and 1:100. The half-life of the nanobubbles at the 1:10 dilution was estimated to be 1.8 ± 0.9 min (n = 3) based on the ultra-harmonic acoustic emission curve (Fig. S1). Besides the first ultra-harmonic emissions (0.75 MHz, 1.5 f0), the acoustic response at other frequencies (0.5 f0, 2 f0, 2.5 f0 and 3 f0) were also evaluated for nanobubbles at those three dilutions, and they all followed the same trend (Fig. S2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: BBB opening using FUS and nanobubbles under real-time feedback control
ce:section.ce:para: BBB opening was evaluated in the rat brain using FUS with nanobubbles at three concentrations (1011, 1010 and 109 bubbles/mL) under real-time feedback control. Figure 5 (a–c) shows both the AUC control signal (green solid, left y-axis) and auto-adjusted ultrasound focal pressure (orange circle, right y-axis) as a function of time for nanobubble dilutions 1:1, 1:10 and 100, respectively. The results demonstrated that the AUC signal could be maintained at the target level (red dashed line) for all dilutions and the ultrasound focal pressure was adjusted in a real-time manner based on the AUC values. Table 1 summarized the mean AUC (4893 ± 2029, 4906 ± 2096 and 4864 ± 2366) and mean ultrasound pressure (0.34 ± 0.03, 0.35 ± 0.02 and 0.36 ± 0.03 MPa) across multiple animals and targets for bubble dilutions 1:1, 1:10 and 1:100, respectively. With lower bubble concentration, the control stability slightly degraded and the ultrasound pressure slightly increased. The gross-tissue photos of the perfused brains tested with the bubble dilutions 1:1, 1:10 and 1:100 are shown in Figure 5 (d–f), respectively. The leakage of the EB dye indicated the BBB was successfully opened with all three bubble concentrations (four targets for each concentration). Across all the animals tested, the dye leakage all looked similar by eye, and there was not an obvious relationship between the EB leakage shape nor amount and the bubble concentration visually.

In the recent decade, nanobubbles have been developed as a new type of ultrasound imaging contrast agent (Gao et al. 2017; Perera et al. 2015; Rapoport et al. 2007; Wu et al. 2013; Zheng et al. 2012). Given their small size (100–500 nm), they can reach areas within tissue with higher concentration where microbubbles have limited access or present with very low amounts. They also exhibit unique acoustic properties compared with microbubbles, and several groups including ours have demonstrated the feasibility of opening the BBB with sub-micron sized bubbles (Bing et al. 2018; Fan et al. 2014; Huang et al. 2015). However, comprehensive characterization of nanobubble response to FUS exposures suitable for BBB opening has not been reported, and the optimal parameters are unknown.

The Pluronic polymer has been used in nanoparticle synthesis for biomedical applications for years owing to its relatively nontoxic nature (Cheng et al. 2016, 2017; Kandukuri et al. 2017; Sakai and Alexandridis 2004; Tharmalingam et al. 2008; Yu et al. 2016). In 2010, Exner et al. determined it can be used to control lipid-bubble size and successfully achieved nano-sized Pluronic-stablized bubbles (Krupka et al. 2010). Based on a similar protocol, DBPC-Pluronic nanobubbles were synthesized in this study with a mean size of 280 nm and mean concentration of 1011 bubbles/mL. Compared with conventional microbubbles (2–8 µm, 108–109 bubbles/mL), the size was reduced one order of magnitude and the concentration was increased two to three orders of magnitude.

The resonant frequency of this nanobubble would be estimated around 23 MHz based on diameter, according to the Minnaert resonance eqn (1):
(1)
f =
1
2 π R
(
3 γ
P 0
ρ
)
1 / 2
where R is the radius of the bubble, γ is the polytropic coefficient, P0 is the steady state pressure and ρ is the density of water. However, our results in Figure 3a suggested that the ultra-harmonic signal from these bubbles can be generated and detected with the intact skull when stimulated with ultrasound at 0.5 MHz (much lower than its resonant frequency). Similar to microbubbles, the stimulated acoustic response increased in magnitude with higher ultrasound pressure. Other studies using similar nanobubbles also showed a strong echogenicity/acoustic emissions at ultrasound frequencies of 3.5, 6.2, 8 or 12 MHz (Hernandez et al. 2017; Wu et al. 2013). While the underlying mechanism for this low-frequency response is still not fully understood, the ability of these nanobubbles to respond across such a wide range of US frequency is interesting and could potentially be a beneficial characteristic for low and high frequency FUS exposures.

The acoustic response of these nanobubbles as a function of concentration is somewhat different from what was observed for micron-sized bubbles. As shown in Figure 3b, the ultra-harmonic signal from stimulated nanobubbles is not affected by decreasing the concentration until a 1:2000 dilution (from 1011–108 bubbles/mL). Most of the assays (2 out of 3) showed a signal increase within that concentration range. It's unclear whether this change is due to the high bubble number (1011 bubbles/mL) or due to the nanobubble's own properties, or both. The persistence of these nanobubbles at original concentration is relatively long (approximately 7 min); however they degraded quickly as the concentration dropped (Fig. 3c). After a dilution of 1000 fold (108 bubbles/mL), the persistence dropped to a level similar to commercially available microbubbles (10–25 s, 108 bubbles/mL) (Bing et al. 2018). This indicated that the concentration plays a significant role in ultrasound persistence. One possibility is that each ultrasound pulse destroys a small portion of bubble agents, therefore a higher concentration provides longer persistence. The nanobubbles' stimulated response is stable for 2 h across a 10-fold range of dilutions. The acoustic emission significantly decreases after 1 h for samples with concentration lower than 109 bubbles/mL (1:100 diluted samples, p < 0.0001). These results provide guidance on how to best select nanobubble concentration and sample preparation time to ensure a stable acoustic response throughout the experiment.

The similar in vivo acoustic response of nanobubbles as a function of ultrasound pressure at 1:1, 1:10 and 1:100 dilutions indicated that there is a range of concentrations that can be employed without sacrificing the absolute acoustic signal strength (Fig. 4a). The mechanism underlying this trend with concentration is not clear and deserves further study. The selection of concentration may be driven by physiologic considerations (tolerability/toxicity) rather than acoustic considerations. It is worth noting that the in vivo AUC values at each corresponding pressure were higher than those in vitro (Fig. 3a), which can be attributed to an increased number of bubbles within the sonicated volume in the brain compared with a small single tube. The broadband emission for 1:10 nanobubbles appears after 0.42 MPa as indicated in the acoustic frequency spectrum over pressure (Fig. 4c). After calculation, significant inertial cavitation for nanobubbles (1:10 samples, as an example) was generated for pressures larger than 0.5 MPa (Fig. 4b, p = 0.02 compared with baseline). Based on the broadband emission results, the AUC control threshold for BBB opening was selected to be 5000 (corresponding pressure: approximately 0.3–0.4 MPa, Fig. 4a) to ensure a stable and clean ultra-harmonic emission while avoiding any inertial cavitation. There are four likely factors that potentially contribute to the long in vivo circulation time of nanobubbles compared with conventional microbubbles under ultrasound exposures suitable for BBB opening (Fig. 4d and Fig. S3). First, it is more efficient for a nanometer-scale bubble to pass through pulmonary capillary beds and the red pulp meshwork of the spleen compared with large diameter microbubbles. Second, it would take more time for the body to completely clear out the bubbles with an increased initial concentration. Third, the polyethylene glycol (PEG) chain and Pluronic polymer on the bubble surface have been found to decrease the uptake of the nanoparticles by macrophages (Wu et al. 2013). Fourth, it was shown previously that bubbles made of lipids with a longer acyl chain length (e.g., DBPC [C22:0] vs. DPPC [C16:0]) tend to have a better stability and longer in vivo circulation time (Garg et al. 2013). It is worth noting that for the highest bubble concentration (1:1, 1011 bubbles/mL), there were two peaks after a bolus injection (Fig. 4d and Fig. S2). The first peak was believed to be the first pass of bubbles into the brain, and the second gradual peak was the result of recirculation due to high bubble population similar to typical computed tomogaphy contrast agent dynamic curves (O'Connor et al. 2011; Violon 2012). The longer in vivo circulation time of these nanobubbles is favorable for BBB opening. Practical implications of this finding would be a reduction in the number of bubble injections required to treat a large region of tissue. Note that the circulation time curves in this study were based on ultra-harmonic emissions under ultrasound exposures suitable for BBB opening (10 ms burst, 1 Hz repetition frequency). While different from common time-intensity curves obtained from ultrasound contrast images used for half-life calculation, this better represents the time-course of the feedback signal in this control application.

The acoustic feedback signal was well maintained at the target AUC level for nanobubbles at 1:1, 1:10 and 1:100 dilutions throughout the whole BBB opening experiment (Fig. 5a–c). This performance was repeatable across multiple animals and targets (Table 1); however, the signal to noise ratio decreased with lower bubble concentration. The EB leakage in the ultrasound targets shown in Figure 5 (d–f) indicated that successful BBB opening was achieved for all of them, even though the control signal variability was slightly larger for the 1:100 dilution. The ultrasound focal pressure applied to achieve the desired AUC level was around 0.35 MPa for all dilutions (Table 1). This result suggested that the pressure required to achieve a target level of acoustic emissions is independent of the nanobubble concentration at a sonicating frequency of 0.5 MHz and for this nanobubble concentration range. In addition, this pressure is closer to the lower range of what is currently considered a safe pressure to open the BBB (Chopra et al. 2010; McDannold et al. 2008b). This improves the safety profile of nanobubbles-mediated BBB opening. Previous studies observed that a higher ultrasound pressure was required to open the BBB for smaller microbubbles (>0.46 MPa for 1–2 µm bubbles) (Choi et al. 2010; Tung et al. 2011). Our results suggested this was not the case for these nanobubbles. These differences may be attributable to the nanoscale size of the bubbles, their lipid formulation or their particle number. Also worth mentioning is that only the treatment duration of 100 s was investigated in this study, but early testing suggested the BBB opening can be achieved with even shorter durations. Besides that, this study used 100% oxygen as the anesthesia carrier gas, which may cause a diminution of BBB disruption (McDannold et al. 2017). Subsequent studies should investigate optimal exposure durations and carrier gas to achieve BBB opening with an acceptable safety profile.

This work has several limitations. First, although the BBB opening using nanobubbles at different concentrations was demonstrated, the amount of agent delivery across the BBB for the different bubble concentrations was not quantified. Second, besides bubble concentration, there are several additional parameters that should be investigated to further optimize BBB opening with nanobubbles (e.g., treatment duration, times and frequency). Third, a comprehensive safety evaluation was not included in this study and will be the subject of a future investigation. Finally, the nanobubbles used in this study are not approved for human use. However, if the properties of BBB opening with nanobubbles are favorable to microbubbles, the effort to translate them into clinical use would be warranted.

The harmonic response of stimulated nanobubbles was measurable at concentrations ranging from 107–1011 bubbles/mL. Evaluation and characterization of the nanobubbles’ acoustic responses were performed both in vitro and in vivo. The circulation time in rats was measured to be approximately 10 min with undiluted nanobubbles (1011 bubbles/mL) and reduced to 5–6 min with 1:10 and 1:100 diluted nanobubbles. All three tested dilutions (1:1, 1:10 and 1:100) were able to open the BBB under real-time feedback control with a reasonable signal-to-noise ratio. The opening effect of nanobubbles across different concentrations was robust and reliable.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by funding from the Cancer Prevention and Research Initiative of Texas (CPRIT) R1308 (RC). The authors would like to acknowledge Debra Szczepanski for animal technical assistance. The authors also thank Dr. Jinming Gao, Dr. Caroline Lux and Mr. William Grozinger for sharing their equipment and their assistance in bubble size and concentration measurements.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.ultrasmedbio.2019.03.016.
